name: Society for Psychical Research (SPR)
description: The oldest and most prominent psychical research society in the world, dedicated to the scientific investigation of psychic phenomena.

name: American Society for Psychical Research (ASPR)
description: The oldest psychical research organization in the United States, conducting scientific research into psychic phenomena including ESP, telepathy, and survival after death.

name: Parapsychological Association (PA)
description: The international professional organization of scientists and scholars engaged in the study of psi phenomena. Affiliated with the American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS).

name: Rhine Research Center
description: An independent research institution continuing the work begun at the Duke University Parapsychology Laboratory, focusing on the science of consciousness and anomalous phenomena.

name: Institute of Noetic Sciences (IONS)
description: A nonprofit organization that conducts and sponsors research on the nature and potential of consciousness, exploring the interconnection between science and human experience.

name: Koestler Parapsychology Unit
description: A research unit at the University of Edinburgh that conducts scientific investigations into parapsychological phenomena, one of the few academic parapsychology units still active.

name: Division of Perceptual Studies (DOPS)
description: A research unit within the Department of Psychiatry at the University of Virginia that investigates phenomena such as near-death experiences, past-life memories, and apparitions.

name: Duke University Parapsychology Laboratory
description: Founded by J.B. Rhine and William McDougall, this laboratory pioneered laboratory-based parapsychology research including the famous card-guessing experiments and coinage of the term 'extrasensory perception.'
notes: Predecessor to the Rhine Research Center; formally closed as part of Duke University in 1965

name: Princeton Engineering Anomalies Research (PEAR) Lab
description: A research program at Princeton University that studied the potential influence of human consciousness on physical systems such as random event generators. Operated for nearly three decades before closing.
notes: Closed 2007; civilian academic research contextually linked to the government-funded Stargate era

name: Maimonides Medical Center Dream Lab
description: A sleep and dream research laboratory that conducted pioneering controlled studies on dream telepathy, making it one of the first settings to apply rigorous experimental methods to anomalous dream phenomena.

name: Freiburg Institute for Frontier Areas of Psychology
description: A German research institute that investigated anomalous psychological phenomena including parapsychology, altered states of consciousness, and related frontier areas of science.

name: Journal of Parapsychology
description: One of the primary peer-reviewed publications in the field, founded in 1937 at Duke University. Covers experimental and theoretical research in parapsychology.

name: Journal of the Society for Psychical Research
description: The official publication of the Society for Psychical Research, featuring research articles, case reports, and reviews on psychical phenomena since 1884.

name: Journal of Scientific Exploration
description: A peer-reviewed journal covering topics at the boundaries of mainstream science, including anomalous cognition, UAP research, and related frontier subjects.

name: European Journal of Parapsychology
description: A peer-reviewed journal that published parapsychological research primarily from European researchers, covering experimental, theoretical, and historical perspectives.

name: Journal of Consciousness Studies
description: An interdisciplinary journal dedicated to the study of consciousness, with overlapping interest in mind-body questions, anomalous experience, and the hard problem of consciousness.

name: U.S. Stargate Project
description: A classified U.S. government program that researched remote viewing and related psychic phenomena for potential intelligence applications. Funded by the Defense Intelligence Agency and CIA, running from the early 1970s until 1995.

name: SRI International (Psi-Related Contracts)
description: Stanford Research Institute (now SRI International) conducted government-funded research into remote viewing and related psychic phenomena as part of the Stargate program, with researchers including Hal Puthoff and Russell Targ.

name: National Aviation Reporting Center on Anomalous Phenomena (NARCAP)
description: A scientific research organization focused specifically on aviation-related UAP encounters. NARCAP maintains the most comprehensive database of pilot-reported UAP, advocates for reduced reporting stigma in the aviation community, and publishes technical analyses of specific pilot encounters and survey data on underreporting rates.

name: Scientific Coalition for UAP Studies (SCU)
description: A multidisciplinary research organization founded in 2017 that has published peer-reviewed technical analyses of specific UAP cases including the USS Nimitz encounters and the Aguadilla, Puerto Rico infrared footage. SCU methodology emphasises detailed physical and sensor analysis.

name: The Galileo Project
description: A scientific initiative founded by Harvard astronomer Avi Loeb in 2021 to collect systematic, calibrated observational data on UAP using purpose-built sensor arrays incorporating optical, infrared, radio, and seismic instruments. Represents the most rigorous current effort to move UAP research onto a standard scientific data-collection footing.
notes: Harvard University; Avi Loeb, principal investigator

name: Mutual UFO Network (MUFON)
description: The largest civilian UAP investigative organization by membership, MUFON maintains a global network of trained field investigators and a database of tens of thousands of case reports. Publishes the MUFON UFO Journal.

name: All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO)
description: The primary U.S. government body for UAP investigation, established within the Office of the Secretary of Defense in July 2022. AARO's mandate covers aerial, sub-surface, and near-space anomalous phenomena. Required by statute to report annually to Congress and to maintain secure reporting mechanisms for government personnel.

name: UAP Task Force (UAPTF)
description: Established within the Office of Naval Intelligence in August 2020, the UAPTF produced the Preliminary Assessment: Unidentified Aerial Phenomena (June 2021) — the first official public UAP analysis report since Project Blue Book. Assessed 144 cases from government sources, concluding 143 were unexplained.

name: Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP)
description: A classified U.S. government program funded from 2007 to 2012 within the Senate's defense budget, administered by Luis Elizondo in the Office of the Under Secretary of Defense for Intelligence. AATIP collected and analysed sensor data from military UAP encounters and contracted studies on advanced aerospace concepts. Its existence was disclosed by the New York Times in December 2017.

name: NICAP (National Investigations Committee on Aerial Phenomena)
description: Founded by retired Navy physicist Thomas Townsend Brown, NICAP was the largest and most influential civilian UAP organization of the 1950s and 1960s under Donald Keyhoe's leadership. Maintained extensive case files and lobbied for congressional investigation of UAP secrecy.
notes: Operated 1956–1980; archives partially preserved by CUFOS

name: CUFOS (Center for UFO Studies)
description: Founded by J. Allen Hynek in 1973, CUFOS is a non-profit scientific research organization that publishes the International UFO Reporter and maintains one of the most extensive historical UAP case databases. Continues the analytical tradition Hynek established during his tenure as Project Blue Book's scientific consultant.

name: The Black Vault
description: An online archive of declassified U.S. government documents, founded by researcher John Greenewald Jr., containing the largest publicly accessible collection of FOIA-acquired UAP-related materials, including the complete digitised Project Blue Book archive and thousands of more recent government releases.

name: GEIPAN (France)
description: The Groupe d'Études et d'Informations sur les Phénomènes Aérospatiaux Non-identifiés, operating under the French space agency CNES since 1977. One of the few national UAP investigation bodies that has operated continuously with public transparency. Publishes its case database online and employs a four-tier classification system; approximately 3% of cases are classified as unexplained after thorough investigation.

name: Chilean CEFAA
description: Chile's Committee for Studies of Anomalous Aerial Phenomena, operating under the Civil Aeronautics Directorate. Has produced several carefully documented analyses of pilot UAP encounters, including the 2014 Chilean Navy helicopter infrared case, and is considered one of the more methodologically rigorous national UAP investigation bodies.
notes: Operates under Chilean Civil Aeronautics Directorate
